seo.title: Lopburi: Complete Travel Guide 2026 — Monkey Temple, Ancient Ruins & Sunflower Fields
seo.metaDescription: Plan your Lopburi trip with our complete 2026 guide. Phra Prang Sam Yot monkey temple, King Narai's Palace, Khao Chin Lae sunflower fields, best hotels, and practical tips for central Thailand's ancient city.

overview: Lopburi is central Thailand's most historically layered destination — a city where Khmer Hindu shrines from the 9th century, a 17th-century royal palace built for a French-allied king, and thousands of macaque monkeys coexist on the same city block. Known as the 'Monkey City', Lopburi's long-tailed macaques have taken over Phra Prang Sam Yot (the three-spired Khmer temple in the town center) and the adjacent San Phra Kan shrine, treating the ruins as their personal playground. Beyond the monkeys, Lopburi was the second capital of the Ayutthaya Kingdom under King Narai the Great (1656-1688), who built a vast palace complex and welcomed French diplomats and Jesuit priests — creating an extraordinary blend of Thai, Khmer, and European Baroque architecture unique in all of Thailand. The city sits 153 km north of Bangkok, making it an ideal day trip or overnight stop on the route to Chiang Mai. From November to January, the surrounding countryside erupts in sunflower fields at Khao Chin Lae, adding a spectacular seasonal attraction to the city's ancient core.
highlights: Phra Prang Sam Yot (Monkey Temple) — three 13th-century Khmer-style prangs overrun by hundreds of long-tailed macaques, one of Thailand's most bizarre and memorable sights, 50 THB entrance
Phra Narai Ratchaniwet Palace — King Narai's 17th-century royal palace complex with French-influenced architecture, on-site national museum, and beautiful grounds, 150 THB entrance
Khao Chin Lae Sunflower Fields — vast fields of sunflowers against the backdrop of Khao Chin Lae Mountain, spectacular November to January, 20 THB entrance
Wat Phra Sri Rattana Mahathat — a 12th-century Khmer-era temple with a massive prang and beautiful Buddha images, walkable from the train station, 50 THB entrance
Ban Wichayen (Vichayen House) — a 17th-century European-style residence built for the French ambassador, one of the most unusual colonial buildings in Thailand, included in 150 THB combo ticket
Phra Prang Khaek (San Phra Kan) — the oldest Khmer Hindu shrine in central Thailand, dating to the 9th century, free entrance with monkey encounters guaranteed
Lopburi Monkey Banquet Festival — held annually on the last Sunday of November, a surreal feast laid out for thousands of monkeys in the old town
Wat Khao Chin Lae — hilltop temple with sweeping mountain views and peacocks, combine with the sunflower fields below
Bala Hisaar (Chao Phraya Wichayen fort ruins) — atmospheric ruins of a fort built for Greek advisor Constantine Phaulkon who held remarkable power in the Ayutthaya court
Lopburi night markets — the old town night market (daily from 4 PM) and the Wednesday Walking Street on Phraya Kamchat Street, serving cheap local food at authentic prices

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Nov-Feb: Cool dry season with the best weather for sightseeing. November coincides with the Monkey Banquet Festival (last Sunday) and the start of the sunflower bloom at Khao Chin Lae. December-January sees the sunflower fields at their peak. Temperatures are comfortable (22-32C), skies are clear, and this is the ideal time for exploring the outdoor ruins and palace grounds.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: Mar-Apr: Still dry but increasingly hot (35-40C). Fewer tourists than peak season, hotel prices are lower. Songkran in April brings a festive atmosphere. The heat makes midday outdoor sightseeing uncomfortable — plan early morning starts for the ruins and palace. Sunflower fields are gone by February.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: May-Oct: Hot and humid with regular afternoon thunderstorms. The ruins and palace can get muddy after rain, but the covered temple areas remain accessible. Hotel prices drop 20-30%. The monkeys are always present year-round — rain doesn't stop them.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit November to February for the best combination of weather, the sunflower fields, and the Monkey Banquet Festival. Lopburi makes an excellent day trip from Bangkok year-round — the train takes only 2-3 hours each way. For the sunflower fields, plan specifically for November to January and arrive early on weekends when the fields get crowded with Thai tourists.

topAttractions.name: Phra Prang Sam Yot (Monkey Temple)
topAttractions.description: The defining image of Lopburi — three connected 13th-century Khmer-style laterite prangs (tower-shrines) rising above the busy old town, swarming with hundreds of long-tailed macaques. The temple was originally Hindu (dedicated to the Trimurti), later converted to Buddhist use by the Thais. The monkeys have completely colonized the ruins, climbing the prangs, swinging from the gate, and stealing anything shiny from tourists. A surreal and unforgettable sight unlike any other in Thailand.
topAttractions.location: Wichayen Road, Old Town Lopburi — directly opposite the train station
topAttractions.entranceFee: 50 THB foreigners (10 THB Thais). Part of the 150 THB combo ticket covering 4 sites.
tips: Keep all bags zipped and remove sunglasses before entering — monkeys will grab both
Staff sell sticks (10 THB) to wave off aggressive monkeys — worth it
Best photographed in the early morning (7-9 AM) with fewer tourists and better light

topAttractions.name: Phra Narai Ratchaniwet Palace
topAttractions.description: King Narai the Great's 17th-century royal palace — a sprawling complex of throne halls, ceremonial buildings, and formal gardens built between 1665-1677 with the help of French and Italian architects. The unusual hybrid architecture blends Ayutthaya Thai, Khmer, and European Baroque elements, reflecting Narai's famous alliance with Louis XIV of France. The on-site Lopburi National Museum houses an excellent collection of Lopburi-period Buddhas, Khmer artifacts, and exhibits on King Narai's reign.
topAttractions.location: Sorasak Road, Old Town Lopburi — central location accessible on foot from the train station
topAttractions.entranceFee: 150 THB foreigners (includes Narai Ratchaniwet Museum). Also available as part of 150 THB combo ticket.
tips: Open Wednesday to Sunday 8:30 AM-4:00 PM (grounds open to 5:30 PM) — closed Monday and Tuesday
Arrive when it opens to avoid tour groups from Bangkok
The museum inside the palace grounds is excellent — budget 1.5-2 hours for both palace and museum

topAttractions.name: Khao Chin Lae Sunflower Fields
topAttractions.description: A spectacular seasonal attraction approximately 40 km north of Lopburi city — vast fields of sunflowers covering the hillsides around Khao Chin Lae Mountain with a hilltop temple (Wat Khao Chin Lae) offering panoramic views over the golden carpet below. The combination of sunflower fields, mountain backdrop, and Buddhist temple makes for extraordinary photography. This is one of the most visited natural attractions in central Thailand during the blooming season.
topAttractions.location: Khao Chin Lae, approximately 40 km north of Lopburi city. Take Highway 21 north toward Chai Badan.
tips: Fields bloom from mid-November to January — peak color is usually December
Visit on weekday mornings to avoid massive Thai tourist crowds on weekends
The hilltop temple Wat Khao Chin Lae has peacocks wandering the grounds and great views

topAttractions.name: Wat Phra Sri Rattana Mahathat
topAttractions.description: Lopburi's most impressive single temple — a large 12th-century Khmer-period complex dominated by a massive central prang (tower) surrounded by smaller towers and mandapas. The prang has been restored but retains much of its original laterite stonework. Beautiful stucco Buddhas and Lopburi-period sculpture decorate the niches. Located right next to the train station, it's the first major ruin most visitors encounter.
topAttractions.location: Na Phra Kan Road, adjacent to Lopburi train station
topAttractions.entranceFee: 50 THB foreigners (part of 150 THB combo ticket)
tips: Combined ticket (150 THB) covers this temple plus Prang Sam Yot, Ban Wichayen, and Kraison Siharat Palace
The temple grounds are pleasant for early morning exploration before the heat builds
Look for the Khmer-period Buddha images in the niches — distinctive Lopburi style with ornate headdresses

topAttractions.name: Ban Wichayen (Vichayen House)
topAttractions.description: A fascinatingly odd piece of 17th-century history — a European-style residence built by King Narai in 1685 for Constantine Phaulkon, a Greek adventurer who rose to become one of the most powerful officials in the Ayutthaya Kingdom, and to receive the French ambassador from Louis XIV. The complex of colonial-style buildings includes a Roman Catholic church facade, European reception halls, and Thai-style quarters. The French-Thai diplomatic history visible in these ruins is genuinely unique in Southeast Asia.
topAttractions.location: Wichayen Road, Old Town Lopburi — 300m south of Phra Narai Ratchaniwet Palace
topAttractions.entranceFee: 50 THB foreigners (part of 150 THB combo ticket)
tips: Read about Constantine Phaulkon before visiting — his story makes the ruins far more interesting
The Catholic church facade is the most photogenic part of the complex

topAttractions.name: Phra Prang Khaek (San Phra Kan Shrine)
topAttractions.description: The oldest surviving Khmer structure in central Thailand — three brick Hindu shrine towers dating to the 9th-10th century CE, built during the early Angkorian period and dedicated to the Hindu Trimurti (Brahma, Vishnu, Shiva). The shrine is still actively worshipped by local residents who make offerings to the four-armed Vishnu image. The adjacent San Phra Kan shrine is a major monkey hangout — often with more macaques than Prang Sam Yot. Free to enter, so it's an easy first stop.
topAttractions.location: Na Phra Kan Road, Old Town Lopburi — opposite the train station roundabout
tips: Monkeys are very active here — keep food in sealed bags
The shrine is still an active place of worship — be respectful
Visit together with the nearby Prang Sam Yot (50m away) in the same morning

travelTips: Buy the 150 THB combo ticket covering Wat Phra Sri Mahathat, Prang Sam Yot, Ban Wichayen, and Kraison Siharat Palace — it saves money if visiting more than two sites
Lopburi is easily visited as a day trip from Bangkok — trains take 2-3 hours and run frequently from Hua Lamphong and Krung Thep Aphiwat station
Store valuables in your hotel before walking around the monkey areas — macaques are bold thieves and will snatch glasses, earrings, water bottles, and phones
The old town is compact and walkable — most historical sites are within 1 km of the train station
For the sunflower fields, rent a motorbike (200-250 THB/day) in Lopburi city — they're 40 km away and not accessible by public transport
The Monkey Banquet Festival (last Sunday of November) draws huge crowds — book accommodation and trains well in advance
New Town (east of the railway line) has better hotels but fewer historical sights — a tuk-tuk between areas costs 40-60 THB
Lopburi's covered markets and shaded ruins make it more bearable than open-air sites during the hot season (March-May)
gettingThere: Lopburi is 153 km north of Bangkok. Train is the best option — multiple daily departures from Bangkok's Hua Lamphong and Krung Thep Aphiwat stations, journey time 2.5-3 hours, tickets from 28-338 THB depending on class. The train station in the Old Town puts you walking distance from all major attractions. Buses from Bangkok's Mo Chit Bus Station take 3-4 hours (80-100 THB). Air-conditioned minivans from Mo Chit BTS station take 2.5-3 hours (120-150 THB). There is no airport in Lopburi — the nearest airport is in Bangkok (Don Mueang or Suvarnabhumi).
gettingAround: The Old Town is very walkable — Prang Sam Yot, Phra Narai Palace, Ban Wichayen, and Wat Phra Sri Mahathat are all within 500m of each other. Songthaews (shared pickup trucks) run along Wichayen Road and Narai Maharat Road for a flat 10 THB fare, connecting Old Town to New Town. Tuk-tuks are available for 40-80 THB for short trips. Motorbike rental (200-250 THB/day) is essential for reaching Khao Chin Lae sunflower fields (40 km away). There are no Grab or Bolt services in Lopburi — negotiate tuk-tuk fares before boarding.
